en.extract:
Lina's sunbird (Aethopyga linaraborae) is a species of bird in the family Nectariniidae   It is endemic to mountains in the island of Mindanao in the Philippines. It is one of the most striking sunbirds in the country with the male having an iridescent blue color and an orange spot on its yellow breast. It is named after Dioscoro S. Rabor's wife, Lina who would assist Dioscoro on expeditions. Its natural habitat is tropical moist montane forest above 1000m. It is threatened by habitat loss.


Description

This species was formerly conspecific with the Apo sunbird but was split by Robert Kennedy, Pedro Gonzales and Hector Miranda in 1997. They decided to name this species after Lina Rabor whose husband is Dioscoro Rabor who is known for as the "Father of Philippine Biology and Conservation" and described 69 bird species


Ecology and behavior
This species has not yet been comprehensively studied. It is presumed to have a typical sunbird diet of primarily nectar and supplemented with the occasional insect. 
Males in breeding condition with enlarged testes have been collected n May but this is not yet fully understood.


Habitat and conservation status
It occupies montane mossy forest from 970 to 2,000 m and above in the mountains of Eastern Mindanao. 
IUCN has assessed this bird as near threatened making it the only Philippine sunbird that is not assessed as least concern as it r. It is restricted toDespite its limited range, it is said to be locally common in its range. As it occurs in rugged and inaccessible mountains, this has allowed a large portion of its habitat to remain intact. However, there it is still affected by habitat loss through deforestation, mining,  land conversion and slash-and-burn - just not to the same extent as lowland forest.  Mt. Tagubud in New Bataan, where it is mainly seen by birdwatchers, has faced considerable deforestation.

'''Lina's sunbird''' ('''''Aethopyga linaraborae''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Nectariniidae]]   It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to mountains in the island of [[Mindanao]] in the [[Philippines]]. It is one of the most striking [[sunbird]]s in the country with the male having an iridescent blue color and an orange spot on its yellow breast. It is named after [[Dioscoro S. Rabor]]'s wife, Lina who would assist Dioscoro on expeditions.<ref>{{Cite Q|Q104115278|quotation=Lina N. Florendo Rabor accompanied her husband, the preeminent Philippine ornithologist Dioscoro S. Rabor, on more than 40 scientific expeditions throughout the Philippines from 1936 to 1975 and contributed to some 80 papers and books published by Dr. Rabor and his colleagues. Throughout this extraordinary effort she remained a silent partner. We are pleased to name this new and beautiful sunbird after this remarkable person}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]] is [[tropical]] moist [[montane forest]] above 1000m. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

== Description ==
[[File:Aethopyga linaraborae 2009 stamp of the Philippines.jpg|left|thumb|228x228px|A Philippine stamp in 2009 depicting the Lina's sunbird]]
This species was formerly conspecific with the [[Apo sunbird]] but was split by Robert Kennedy, Pedro Gonzales and Hector Miranda in 1997. They decided to name this species after Lina Rabor whose husband is Dioscoro Rabor who is known for as the "Father of Philippine Biology and Conservation" and described 69 bird species

== Ecology and behavior ==
This species has not yet been comprehensively studied. It is presumed to have a typical sunbird diet of primarily nectar and supplemented with the occasional insect. 

Males in breeding condition with enlarged testes have been collected n May but this is not yet fully understood.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cheke |first1=Robert |last2=Mann |first2=Clive |date=2020 |title=Lina's Sunbird (Aethopyga linaraborae), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/linsun1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.linsun1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
It occupies montane mossy forest from 970 to 2,000 m and above in the mountains of Eastern Mindanao. 

IUCN has assessed this bird as [[near threatened]] making it the only Philippine sunbird that is not assessed as least concern as it r. It is restricted toDespite its limited range, it is said to be locally common in its range. As it occurs in rugged and inaccessible mountains, this has allowed a large portion of its habitat to remain intact. However, there it is still affected by [[habitat loss]] through deforestation, mining,  [[land conversion]] and [[slash-and-burn]] - just not to the same extent as lowland forest.  Mt. Tagubud in [[New Bataan]], where it is mainly seen by [[Birdwatcher|birdwatchers]], has faced considerable [[deforestation]].
